title: A History of Freedom of Thought
description: A History of Freedom of Thought — knowledge fragments for AI agents

memes.label: About This Wiki
memes.description: A knowledge wiki for "A History of Freedom of Thought" — this page is auto-generated from book metadata

memes.label: 'Table of Contents'
memes.description: 'Complete table of contents for J.B. Bury''s A History of Freedom of Thought (1913), with chapter summaries.'

relations.label: Freedom of Thought and the Forces Against It
The Justification of Liberty of Thought

memes.label: "Agnosticism"
memes.description: "The philosophical position that the existence of God or ultimate reality is unknown and probably unknowable—a middle way between theism and atheism."

memes.label: Algernon Charles Swinburne
memes.description: English poet whose fearless utterances against the tyranny of Gods and Governments, expressed in works such as Atalanta in Calydon (1865) and Songs before Sunrise (1871), made him a leading literary champion of atheism and revolution in Victorian England.

memes.label: "Anabaptists"
memes.description: "A radical Reformation movement advocating adult baptism, separation of church and state, and religious liberty, whose persecution and ideas contributed significantly to the development of freedom of thought."

memes.label: "Anaxagoras"
memes.description: "Ionian Greek philosopher who taught that the sun was a mass of flaming matter, prosecuted for blasphemy in Athens but saved by Pericles—an early case of the conflict between rational inquiry and religious orthodoxy."

memes.label: Anthony Collins
memes.description: English country gentleman and disciple of John Locke, whose Discourse of Free-thinking (1713) and Discourse on the Grounds and Reasons of the Christian Religion (1733) challenged revelation and argued for free rational inquiry into religious questions.

memes.label: "Anthropology"
memes.description: "The scientific study of human societies, cultures, and origins, which in the nineteenth and early twentieth centuries provided powerful evidence against traditional religious claims about human origins and the uniqueness of Christian revelation."

memes.label: "Aristotle"
memes.description: "Ancient Greek philosopher whose works became foundational to medieval Christian theology through Scholasticism, creating a lasting tension between reason and faith in Western thought."

memes.label: "Atheism"
memes.description: "The absence of belief in the existence of deities, and its historical role in the development of freedom of thought."

memes.label: Auguste Comte
memes.description: French philosopher (1798–1857) who founded Positivism, rejecting theology and metaphysics in favor of science, and invented the Religion of Humanity.
aliases: Isidore Auguste Marie François Xavier Comte

memes.label: Augustine of Hippo
memes.description: Early Christian theologian (354–430), the most esteemed Church Father who formulated the theological principle of religious persecution based on the Gospel text "Compel them to come in," shaping Western Christianity's attitude toward dissent for centuries.

memes.label: "Authority"
memes.description: "The principle of accepting beliefs on the basis of external testimony, tradition, or institutional decree rather than independent rational inquiry — the central antagonist of reason in Bury's thesis about the history of freedom of thought."

memes.label: "Averroes (Ibn Rushd)"
memes.description: "Twelfth-century Andalusian philosopher and freethinker whose Aristotelian commentaries propagated a wave of rationalism in medieval Christian Europe, advancing the doctrine of double truth."

memes.label: Baron d'Holbach
memes.description: French-German philosopher (1723–1789) whose System of Nature (1770) presented a comprehensive atheistic and materialist worldview, marking a radical turning point in Enlightenment freethought.

memes.label: Baruch Spinoza
memes.description: Dutch Jewish philosopher (1632–1677) whose pantheistic philosophy and biblical criticism constituted a radical breach with orthodox theology, laying foundations for modern rationalism.

memes.label: "Blaise Pascal"
memes.description: "French mathematician, physicist, and religious philosopher whose famous 'wager' argument for Christian belief was revived by later apologists but ultimately undermined rational theology."

memes.label: "Blasphemy"
memes.description: "The crime of speaking or writing disrespectfully about God or sacred things, and its historical use as a tool for suppressing freedom of thought."

memes.label: "Broad Church"
memes.description: "A liberal movement within the Church of England in the mid-nineteenth century that embraced biblical criticism and sought to reconcile Christianity with modern science and scholarship."

memes.label: "Sebastian Castellio"
memes.description: "Sixteenth-century French theologian and advocate of religious toleration who opposed the execution of Michael Servetus and championed liberty of conscience."

memes.label: "Censorship"
memes.description: "The systematic suppression of ideas and publications deemed dangerous by religious or political authorities, and its role in the struggle for freedom of thought."

memes.label: "Chapter I: Freedom of Thought and the Forces Against It"
memes.description: "How social forces and authority have historically opposed freedom of thought, and the distinction between private thought and public speech."

memes.label: "Chapter II: Reason Free (Greece and Rome)"
memes.description: "The rise of rational inquiry in ancient Greece and Rome, and the classical foundations of freethought."

memes.label: "Chapter III: Reason in Prison (The Middle Ages)"
memes.description: "How reason was suppressed during the Middle Ages under ecclesiastical authority and dogma."

memes.label: "Chapter IV: Prospect of Deliverance (The Renaissance and the Reformation)"
memes.description: "The Renaissance and Reformation as turning points that opened the prospect of intellectual deliverance."

memes.label: "Chapter V: Religious Toleration"
memes.description: "The development and limits of religious toleration from antiquity through the Enlightenment."

memes.label: "Chapter VI: The Growth of Rationalism (17th–18th Centuries)"
memes.description: "The growth of rationalism in the 17th and 18th centuries, from the Scientific Revolution to the Enlightenment."

memes.label: "Chapter VII: The Progress of Rationalism (19th Century)"
memes.description: "The progress of rationalism in the 19th century, driven by modern science and historical criticism."

memes.label: "Chapter VIII: The Justification of Liberty of Thought"
memes.description: "The philosophical and political justification of liberty of thought as a fundamental principle of modern society."

memes.label: "Charles Bradlaugh"
memes.description: "British secularist and political activist whose campaigns for free thought, secular education, and the right of atheists to sit in Parliament advanced the cause of freedom of thought in Victorian Britain."

memes.label: "Charles Darwin"
memes.description: "English naturalist whose theory of evolution by natural selection (On the Origin of Species, 1859) fundamentally challenged biblical creation and the argument from design, advancing scientific rationalism against theological orthodoxy."

memes.label: Charles Lyell
memes.description: Scottish geologist (1797–1875) whose *Principles of Geology* (1830–33) undermined the biblical account of creation by demonstrating that the earth's history could be explained by natural processes operating gradually over vast periods of time.

memes.label: Christian Thomasius
memes.description: German jurist and philosopher who, in a series of pamphlets (1693–1697), laid down the principle that religious liberty follows from a right conception of law, arguing that heresy is not a crime but an error.

memes.label: Christopher Marlowe
memes.description: Elizabethan poet and dramatist accused of atheism, killed in a tavern brawl in 1593 before his prosecution could proceed.

memes.label: Cicero
memes.description: Roman statesman, orator, and philosopher (106–43 BCE) whose skeptical view of religion as a social utility and whose rational writings influenced later freethought.

memes.label: "Clergy"
memes.description: "The ordained religious order and its role as both guardian of orthodoxy and object of freethought critique in the history of freedom of thought."

memes.label: W. K. Clifford
memes.description: British mathematician and philosopher (1845–1879) whose essay "Body and Mind" contributed to the late Victorian rationalist movement, alongside the works of Huxley and Tyndall, advancing a naturalistic understanding of consciousness.

memes.label: "Marquis de Condorcet"
memes.description: "French Enlightenment philosopher and mathematician who championed the doctrine of human perfectibility and the indefinite progress of civilization."
aliases: Marie Jean Antoine Nicolas de Caritat

memes.label: Conscience
memes.description: The inner moral faculty claimed as the basis for individual religious judgment — the foundational principle from which freedom of thought historically emerged in Bury's account.

memes.label: Constantine the Great
memes.description: Roman Emperor (c.272–337 CE) who issued the Edict of Toleration, ending the persecution of Christianity and establishing the principle of religious freedom within the Roman Empire.

memes.label: "Conyers Middleton"
memes.description: "English clergyman and scholar, perhaps the ablest of the deists, whose Free Inquiry (1748) challenged the historical basis of Christian miracles and influenced Gibbon."

memes.label: Nicolaus Copernicus
memes.description: Polish astronomer (1473–1543) whose heliocentric theory replaced the Earth with the Sun at the center of the universe, initiating the scientific revolution that transformed the relationship between science and religious authority.

memes.label: "Creeds"
memes.description: "Authoritative formulations of religious doctrine that defined the boundaries of acceptable belief, serving as instruments of intellectual conformity and targets of freethought criticism throughout European history."

memes.label: David Friedrich Strauss
memes.description: German theologian and writer (1808–1874) whose Life of Jesus (1835) rejected the supernatural elements of the Gospels and helped inaugurate modern critical study of the New Testament.

memes.label: "David Hume"
memes.description: "Scottish Enlightenment philosopher whose sceptical critiques of miracles and the argument from design undermined the rational foundations of natural theology, contributing decisively to the cause of freethought."

memes.label: "Deism"
memes.description: "The rationalist religious philosophy that posits God as a divine creator who does not intervene through miracles or revelation, serving as a crucial bridge between orthodox Christianity and freethought."

memes.label: "Democracy"
memes.description: "The system of government by popular rule or representation, and its complex historical relationship with freedom of thought from Athenian democracy to 19th-century liberalism."

memes.label: Democritus
memes.description: Greek philosopher (c. 460–370 BCE) whose atomic theory of the universe made him one of the earliest and most influential rationalist thinkers in the history of freethought.

memes.label: "Denis Diderot"
memes.description: "French Enlightenment philosopher and chief editor of the Encyclopédie, the great collaborative work that disseminated rationalist and anti-clerical ideas across eighteenth-century Europe."

memes.label: "Dogma"
memes.description: "Authoritative religious doctrines declared to be unquestionably true, and the challenge that rationalism and free inquiry posed to dogmatic authority in the development of freedom of thought."

memes.label: "Ecclesiastical Authority"
memes.description: "The institutional power of the Christian Church and its clergy over doctrine, morality, and intellectual life, and the historical struggle to limit ecclesiastical authority in the development of freedom of thought."

memes.label: Education
memes.description: The systematic cultivation of knowledge and reason — presented by Bury as both a product of freedom of thought and a crucial instrument for its further advance, particularly through the movement for secular education.

memes.label: "Edward Gibbon"
memes.description: "English historian whose Decline and Fall of the Roman Empire subjected the rise of Christianity to critical historical analysis for the first time, becoming the most enduring freethinking classic of the eighteenth century."

memes.label: "The Enlightenment"
memes.description: "The eighteenth-century intellectual movement that championed reason, science, and individual liberty against tradition and ecclesiastical authority, forming the culmination of the progress of rationalism described in Bury's history."

memes.label: "Epicureanism"
memes.description: "An ancient Greek school of philosophy founded by Epicurus that, with its anti-religious materialism and goal of freeing minds from fear of the gods, contributed to the early history of rationalism and freethought."

memes.label: Epicurus
memes.description: Ancient Greek philosopher (341–270 BC) whose materialist and anti-religious teachings aimed to free humanity from the fear of the gods, profoundly influencing the rationalist tradition from antiquity through the Enlightenment.

memes.label: "Ernest Renan"
memes.description: "French philosopher and historian (1823–1892) whose sensational 'Life of Jesus' rejected the supernatural and applied historical criticism to the New Testament, costing him his academic chair."

memes.label: Ernst Haeckel
memes.description: German zoologist and professor at Jena (1834–1919), prophet of evolutionary theory who developed Monism as a scientific-materialist worldview challenging traditional religion.

memes.label: "Ethics"
memes.description: "The study of moral principles and right conduct—a domain at the center of debates over whether morality depends on religion or can be derived from reason and human nature alone."

memes.label: Euripides
memes.description: Athenian tragic poet of the 5th century BCE, saturated with modern speculation, whose characters often expressed highly unorthodox views and who was prosecuted for impiety.

memes.label: "Evangelicalism"
memes.description: "A pietistic revival movement within the Anglican Church in the late eighteenth and early nineteenth centuries that, while energizing personal piety, was hostile to the freedom of religious thought and contributed to a period of reaction against rationalism."

memes.label: "Evolution"
memes.description: "The scientific theory of biological evolution by natural selection, and its profound impact on freedom of thought in the nineteenth and twentieth centuries."

memes.label: "Fausto Sozzini (Socinus)"
memes.description: "Italian theologian (1539–1604) who founded Socinianism, a rationalist Unitarian movement that repudiated religious persecution and advocated liberty of conscience."

memes.label: Charles Fourier
memes.description: French utopian socialist (1772–1837) whose extravagant optimism about human perfectibility represented the extreme limit of the nineteenth-century doctrine of progress, anticipating a future of limitless human achievement.

memes.label: Frederic Harrison
memes.description: English jurist and positivist who, in the latter half of the nineteenth century, was one of the most indefatigable workers in the cause of reason against authority, promulgating the principles of Comte's Positive Philosophy in England.

memes.label: "Frederick the Great"
memes.description: "King of Prussia (1740–1786) and enlightened despot who established full religious liberty — the first modern European ruler to do so — and gave protection to persecuted freethinkers across Europe."

memes.label: "Free Inquiry"
memes.description: "The principle that all subjects, including religion, morality, and politics, should be open to unrestricted rational investigation—the refusal of thought to be constrained by authority, tradition, or prescribed conclusions."

memes.label: "Freedom of Speech"
memes.description: "The right to express opinions without censorship or penalty, and its emergence as a foundational principle of intellectual liberty from antiquity to the modern era."

memes.label: "Freethought"
memes.description: "The practice of forming opinions on the basis of reason and evidence, independently of authority, tradition, or established belief — the central subject of J.B. Bury's 1913 classic study."

memes.label: Galileo Galilei
memes.description: Italian astronomer and physicist (1564–1642) whose defense of Copernican heliocentrism brought him into conflict with the Catholic Church, becoming a landmark case in the history of freedom of thought.

memes.label: Georg Wilhelm Friedrich Hegel
memes.description: German idealist philosopher (1770–1831) whose dialectical system represented the culmination of metaphysical rationalism in the early 19th century, influencing both theological and anti-theological thought.

memes.label: Giordano Bruno
memes.description: Italian philosopher, cosmologist, and former Dominican friar (1548–1600) burned at the stake by the Roman Inquisition for his heretical pantheistic philosophy and his defense of Copernican cosmology.

memes.label: Gotthold Ephraim Lessing
memes.description: German poet, playwright, and philosopher (1729–1781) whose drama Nathan the Wise became a landmark literary argument for religious toleration during the Enlightenment.

memes.label: "Hell"
memes.description: "The doctrine of eternal punishment after death, and its historical role as a sanction for enforcing religious conformity and suppressing freedom of thought."

memes.label: "Henry St John, 1st Viscount Bolingbroke"
memes.description: "English statesman and philosopher (1678–1751) whose posthumously published rationalist essays profoundly influenced Voltaire and the French Enlightenment, advocating deism and attacking religious superstition."

memes.label: "Henry Thomas Buckle"
memes.description: "English historian (1821–1862) whose 'History of Civilization in England' applied deterministic principles to history, excluding divine intervention and advancing a scientific conception of human progress."

memes.label: "Heraclitus"
memes.description: "Pre-Socratic Greek philosopher (c. 535–475 BCE) who taught that all things are in constant flux and that stability is an illusion, contributing to the early rationalist tradition."

memes.label: "Herbert Spencer"
memes.description: "British philosopher and sociologist who applied evolutionary theory to society and championed agnosticism, contributing to 19th-century rationalist thought."

memes.label: "Heresy"
memes.description: "The holding of religious beliefs contrary to established church doctrine, and the systematic persecution of heretics as a central obstacle to freedom of thought in European history."

memes.label: "Historical Criticism"
memes.description: "The application of critical historical methods to religious texts and traditions—undermining the authority of Scripture by treating it as a human document subject to the same scrutiny as any other ancient text."

memes.label: "Immortality"
memes.description: "The doctrine that the human soul survives bodily death, and its disputed role in the intellectual history of freedom of thought from antiquity to the 19th century."

memes.label: "Index"
memes.description: "Alphabetical index of names, topics, and movements referenced throughout the book."

memes.label: "Infallibility"
memes.description: "The doctrine that the Bible or the Church is incapable of error, which Bury identifies as a central obstacle to free inquiry and rational criticism of religious texts in the history of freedom of thought."

memes.label: "The Inquisition"
memes.description: "The organized system of searching out heretics founded by Pope Gregory IX around 1233—a powerful engine for the suppression of freedom of religious opinion, unique in history and not abolished until the nineteenth century."

memes.label: "Religious Intolerance"
memes.description: "The refusal to permit religious beliefs or practices differing from established orthodoxy, identified by Bury as the primary practical obstacle to freedom of thought throughout European history."

memes.label: James Mill
memes.description: Scottish utilitarian philosopher whose unbelief was prompted by reading Butler's Analogy, and who, along with Bentham and his son J. S. Mill, preached the principle of the greatest happiness of the greatest number.

memes.label: "James Watt"
memes.description: "Scottish engineer and inventor whose steam engine improvements symbolized the mechanical ingenuity of the 18th century, referenced by Bury in the context of natural theology debates."

memes.label: John Calvin
memes.description: French Protestant Reformer (1509–1564) whose theocracy at Geneva crushed religious liberty, making him the most famous exemplar of Protestant intolerance.

memes.label: John Henry Newman
memes.description: English theologian and cardinal (1801–1890), leader of the Oxford Movement, whose ideas on doctrinal development and relationship with reason shaped nineteenth-century religious thought.

memes.label: "John Milton"
memes.description: "English poet and prose writer whose Areopagitica (1644) was a landmark defence of freedom of the press and argument for liberty of thought."

memes.label: John Morley
memes.description: British Liberal statesman, writer, and editor (1838–1923) whose biographies of French freethinkers and editorship of the Fortnightly Review advanced rationalist thought in Victorian England.

memes.label: John Robert Seeley
memes.description: English historian and essayist whose Ecce Homo (1865) criticized and defied theological authority, provoking outrage from religious conservatives.

memes.label: John Stuart Mill
memes.description: British philosopher (1806–1873) whose On Liberty (1859) provided the reasoned justification of liberty of thought and discussion.

memes.label: John Toland
memes.description: Irish rationalist philosopher (1670–1722) whose book *Christianity Not Mysterious* (1696) drew the logical conclusions of Locke's philosophy and sparked the Deist controversy in England.

memes.label: "Joseph Butler"
memes.description: "English bishop and philosopher whose Analogy of Religion (1736) inadvertently raised more doubts than it resolved, contributing to the deism controversy."

memes.label: Joseph Priestley
memes.description: English chemist, theologian, and political theorist (1733–1804) whose Unitarian writings, scientific achievements, and doctrine of historical progress made him a significant figure in the rationalist tradition of the eighteenth century.

memes.label: "Julian the Apostate"
memes.description: "Roman Emperor (r. 361–363 CE) who sought to revive paganism and proclaimed universal religious toleration, placing Christianity at a temporary disadvantage after Constantine's revolution."

memes.label: "Immanuel Kant"
memes.description: "German Enlightenment philosopher whose critique of natural theology and the argument from design dealt a decisive blow to rational proofs of God's existence."

memes.label: Leslie Stephen
memes.description: British man of letters and agnostic (1832–1904) whose essays subjected orthodox theology to merciless logical scrutiny, arguing that theological doctrines offer only sham solutions.

memes.label: "Liberty of Conscience"
memes.description: "The principle that the individual conscience is sovereign over human law in matters of belief and worship — a claim that, originating with Socrates, became the central thread in the history of freedom of thought."

memes.label: John Locke
memes.description: English philosopher (1632–1704) whose Letter Concerning Toleration laid the intellectual foundation for religious liberty and the separation of church and state.

memes.label: "Lucian of Samosata"
memes.description: "Greek satirist and rhetorician (2nd century AD) whose witty dialogues ridiculed the gods and religious superstition, marking the last great voice of Greek literary freethought."

memes.label: "Lucilio Vanini"
memes.description: "Italian philosopher and freethinker who wandered Europe like Giordano Bruno and was executed for atheism and blasphemy at Toulouse in 1619."

memes.label: "Lucretius"
memes.description: "Roman poet and Epicurean philosopher (1st century BCE) whose poem 'On the Nature of the World' was a landmark of ancient freethought, attacking religion and expounding materialist philosophy."

memes.label: "Marsilius of Padua"
memes.description: "Thirteenth-century Italian political thinker who argued that the Church had no power to employ physical coercion, anticipating later theories of religious toleration."

memes.label: Martin Luther
memes.description: German theologian (1483–1546) whose rebellion against the Papacy launched the Protestant Reformation, though his own doctrine was quite opposed to liberty of conscience and worship.

memes.label: "Materialism"
memes.description: "The philosophical doctrine that matter is the fundamental substance in nature, and its role in challenging religious orthodoxy and promoting freedom of thought."

memes.label: Matthew Tindal
memes.description: English Fellow of All Souls, Oxford, whose Christianity as old as the Creation (1730) attacked revelation from a rationalist standpoint, arguing that the Bible is superfluous since God revealed natural religion to man by reason alone.

memes.label: "Metaphysics"
memes.description: "The branch of philosophy concerned with the fundamental nature of reality, and its evolving relationship with theology and freedom of thought from Descartes to the rise of positivism."

memes.label: "Michael Servetus"
memes.description: "Spanish physician and theologian burned at the stake in Geneva for denying the Trinity, whose execution became a landmark in the history of religious toleration."

memes.label: "Michel de Montaigne"
memes.description: "French Renaissance philosopher whose skeptical essays questioned dogma and authority, representing the broad-mindedness of the Renaissance and paving the way for modern freethought."

memes.label: "Miracles"
memes.description: "Supernatural interventions in the natural order, particularly those recorded in the Gospels, which became a central target of rationalist criticism in the eighteenth-century deist controversy documented by Bury."

memes.label: Michel de Montaigne
memes.description: French Renaissance philosopher and essayist (1533–1592) whose skeptical inquiries into human knowledge undermined dogmatic certainty and contributed to the development of religious toleration.

memes.label: "Morality"
memes.description: "The principles of right conduct, and the historical debate over whether morality depends on religion—a central question in the development of freedom of thought."

memes.label: Natural Religion
memes.description: The belief in a deity knowable through reason and nature rather than revelation — a central concept in Enlightenment critiques of revealed religion and a bridge between orthodox belief and freethought.

memes.label: "Natural Rights"
memes.description: "The philosophical doctrine that individuals possess inherent rights by virtue of their humanity, independent of any government or social contract — a concept that played a foundational role in arguments for freedom of thought and speech but was subjected to philosophical critique by Bury and other thinkers."

memes.label: "Isaac Newton"
memes.description: "English physicist and mathematician whose discovery of gravitation initiated the process of discharging God from the government of the world through unchangeable natural laws."

memes.label: "Orthodoxy"
memes.description: "The enforcement of officially approved religious doctrine, and its role as the primary institutional barrier to freedom of thought in pre-modern Europe."

memes.label: "Pamphlet"
memes.description: "A short printed booklet or treatise that served as a primary medium for the dissemination of freethought, tolerationist arguments, and religious controversy from the Reformation through the Enlightenment."

memes.label: "Pantheism"
memes.description: "The belief that God is identical with the universe or nature, and its role as a bridge between orthodox religion and rationalist philosophy in the development of free thought."

memes.label: Percy Bysshe Shelley
memes.description: English Romantic poet (1792–1822) whose declared atheism and fearless writings against religious and political tyranny made him a symbol of freethought in the nineteenth century.

memes.label: Pericles
memes.description: Athenian statesman (c.495–429 BC) who guided Athens through its Golden Age and whose personal freethinking protected intellectuals from religious persecution.

memes.label: "Persecution"
memes.description: "The systematic mistreatment of individuals or groups for their beliefs, and the central obstacle to freedom of thought throughout Western history."

memes.label: "Peter Annet"
memes.description: "English schoolmaster and early rationalist who was among the few deists punished for popularizing freethought, sentenced to the pillory and hard labor."

memes.label: Pierre Bayle
memes.description: French Protestant philosopher (1647–1706) whose skeptical arguments for toleration and historical-critical method supplied weapons to freethinkers across Europe.

memes.label: "Pierre Charron"
memes.description: "French philosopher and theologian (1541–1603), friend of Montaigne, whose book 'On Wisdom' (1601) separated morality from religion and laid foundations for modern deism and freethought."

memes.label: Pierre Gassendi
memes.description: French philosopher who revived Epicurean materialism in the 17th century and influenced Thomas Hobbes.

memes.label: "Plato"
memes.description: "Ancient Greek philosopher, student of Socrates, whose dialogues preserved and extended the Socratic method of rational inquiry."

memes.label: "Private Judgment"
memes.description: "The principle that each individual has the right to interpret Scripture and determine religious truth for themselves, and its revolutionary impact on freedom of thought from the Reformation to modern liberalism."

memes.label: Progress
memes.description: The idea that human history exhibits a cumulative advance in knowledge, freedom, and well-being — a conviction closely tied to the rise of rationalism and the growth of freedom of thought.

memes.label: "Providence"
memes.description: "The theological doctrine that God actively governs and guides the world through a divine plan, a concept critically examined by Enlightenment freethinkers including Hume, Gibbon, and the Deists."

memes.label: "Public Opinion"
memes.description: "The collective attitudes and beliefs of a society, which historically acted as both a restraint on and a driver of freedom of thought, evolving from a force of conservative repression to a modern climate of tolerance."

memes.label: "Puritanism"
memes.description: "A Protestant reform movement of the sixteenth and seventeenth centuries that sought to purify the Church of England, but whose record on intellectual liberty was marked by a contradiction: fleeing persecution in England, Puritans established equally intolerant theocracies in New England."

memes.label: "Rationalism"
memes.description: "The philosophical doctrine that reason is the foundation of knowledge and the chief means of intellectual progress, playing a central role in the history of freedom of thought."

memes.label: Reason
memes.description: The faculty of logical thought and inference — the central force in the historical struggle between authority and freedom of thought described in Bury's history.

memes.label: The Reformation
memes.description: The 16th-century religious movement that shattered the unity of Western Christendom and, through the principle of private judgment, inadvertently advanced the cause of freedom of thought.

memes.label: "Religion"
memes.description: "The system of belief in and worship of a superhuman controlling power, and its complex relationship with freedom of thought throughout history."

memes.label: Religious Liberty
memes.description: The legal and political freedom to practice any religion or none — the institutional form of the broader freedom of thought that Bury traces through European history from the Reformation to the nineteenth century.

memes.label: The Renaissance
memes.description: The intellectual and cultural movement (14th–16th centuries) that revived classical learning, cultivated critical scholarship, and began the emancipation of reason from ecclesiastical control.

memes.label: René Descartes
memes.description: French philosopher and mathematician (1596–1650) whose rationalist method and exclusion of authority from scientific investigation made him a foundational figure in the development of modern freethought.

memes.label: "Divine Revelation"
memes.description: "The claim that God has disclosed truths to humanity through supernatural means, particularly through Scripture, which became the central point of contention between deists and orthodox Christians in the eighteenth-century debate documented by Bury."

memes.label: Maximilien Robespierre
memes.description: French revolutionary leader (1758–1794) who declared against atheism during the Revolution, instituting the Cult of the Supreme Being as a deistic alternative to both Christianity and materialism.
aliases: Maximilien François Marie Isidore de Robespierre

memes.label: "Roger Bacon"
memes.description: "13th-century English Franciscan friar and philosopher whose scientific investigations led to his long imprisonment, representing the conflict between empirical inquiry and ecclesiastical authority."

memes.label: Jean-Jacques Rousseau
memes.description: Genevan-born French philosopher (1712–1778) whose social and political theories, though controversial, contributed to the growth of freedom in a distinctive way, and whose idea of civil religion influenced experiments in religious establishment.

memes.label: "Salvation"
memes.description: "The Christian doctrine that salvation is attainable exclusively through the true Church, and its role as a theological justification for religious intolerance and suppression of freethought."

memes.label: Girolamo Savonarola
memes.description: Dominican friar and moral reformer (1452–1498) who preached righteous living at Florence, executed under the notoriously profligate Pope Alexander VI — a case illustrating the Catholic Reformation's campaign to repress freedom more effectually.

memes.label: "Science"
memes.description: "The systematic study of the natural world through observation and experiment, and its transformative impact on freedom of thought."

memes.label: "Secularism"
memes.description: "The principle of separating religion from civic affairs and public education—derived from the Humanists, implying skepticism, and advocating that government and society operate independently of religious authority."

memes.label: Anthony Ashley-Cooper, 3rd Earl of Shaftesbury
memes.description: English philosopher and politician (1671–1713) whose writings on virtue, religion, and ridicule shaped early Enlightenment debates on morality and the use of wit in religious criticism.

memes.label: "Skepticism"
memes.description: "The philosophical tradition of questioning the possibility of certain knowledge, which played a crucial role in challenging religious dogma and advancing freedom of thought."

memes.label: "Socinianism"
memes.description: "A rationalist theological movement founded by Fausto Sozzini (Socinus) in the sixteenth century that was among the first Christian sects to advocate complete religious toleration, deeply influencing the development of liberty of conscience."

memes.label: Socrates
memes.description: Greek philosopher (c. 470–399 BCE) whose trial and death made him a foundational martyr for freedom of thought.

memes.label: "Sophists"
memes.description: "A group of itinerant teachers and educators in fifth-century BCE Greece who trained young men in the use of reason for public life, marking one of the earliest systematic challenges to traditional religious authority and preparing the ground for Greek rationalism."

memes.label: "Stoicism"
memes.description: "An ancient Greek school of philosophy that made notable contributions to freedom of thought through its doctrines of natural law and individual rights against public authority."

memes.label: Superstition
memes.description: Irrational belief in supernatural causation — identified by Bury as one of the chief obstacles to freedom of thought and a target of rationalist critique from antiquity through the Enlightenment.

memes.label: "Theism"
memes.description: "The belief in a personal God or gods who govern the universe, and its evolving relationship with the development of freedom of thought from antiquity to modernity."

memes.label: "Theocracy"
memes.description: "A form of government in which religious authority directly exercises or controls civil power, historically a major obstacle to freedom of thought and a principal target of advocates for the separation of church and state."

memes.label: "Theodosius I"
memes.description: "Roman Emperor (379–395 CE) whose severe laws shattered paganism and established Christian orthodoxy as the state religion, marking a pivotal moment in the history of intellectual freedom."

memes.label: "Theology"
memes.description: "The systematic study of the nature of God and religious belief, and the efforts of freethinkers to free intellectual inquiry from theological constraints."

memes.label: "Thomas Hobbes"
memes.description: "English philosopher whose materialist philosophy and political theory challenged religious authority, making him perhaps the most brilliant English thinker of the 17th century."

memes.label: "Thomas Henry Huxley"
memes.description: "English biologist and educator known as 'Darwin's Bulldog' for his defence of evolutionary theory; coined the term 'agnostic' and championed scientific naturalism against theological orthodoxy."

memes.label: Thomas Paine
memes.description: British-American political writer (1737–1809) whose Rights of Man and The Age of Reason made deist and democratic arguments accessible to ordinary readers, facing prosecution for both.

memes.label: "Thomas Woolston"
memes.description: "English deist and Fellow of Sidney Sussex College, Cambridge, whose Discourses on the Miracles of our Saviour (1727–1730) subjected Gospel miracles to commonsense criticism and resulted in his imprisonment and death, making him one of the few early rationalists to suffer martyrdom for freethought."

memes.label: "Religious Toleration"
memes.description: "The principle that individuals and groups should be permitted to hold and practice their religious beliefs without state persecution, a cornerstone achievement of freethought and rationalism."

memes.label: "Tradition"
memes.description: "The body of inherited customs, beliefs, and institutional practices that historically served as a principal barrier to free inquiry and rational criticism in religion, philosophy, and politics."

memes.label: Anne Robert Jacques Turgot
memes.description: French economist and statesman (1727–1781) who, in 1750, originated the doctrine of historical progress as the organic principle of history, a founding idea of the Enlightenment that transformed how humanity understood its own development.

memes.label: "Unitarianism"
memes.description: "A rationalistic Christian theological movement that rejected the doctrine of the Trinity, emphasized individual judgment in scriptural interpretation, and became one of the earliest and most influential advocates of religious toleration in post-Reformation Europe."

memes.label: Voltaire
memes.description: French Enlightenment writer (1694–1778) whose campaign against religious intolerance and advocacy of deism made him the most influential voice for freedom of thought in 18th-century Europe.

memes.label: W. E. H. Lecky
memes.description: Irish historian (1838–1903) whose History of the Rise and Influence of the Spirit of Rationalism in Europe documented the intellectual foundations of freethought.

memes.label: William Paley
memes.description: English clergyman and philosopher (1743–1805) whose natural theology, especially the watchmaker argument, became the most influential defense of Christianity from design in the late 18th century.

memes.label: "Witchcraft"
memes.description: "The belief in witchcraft and demonic magic whose persecution and eventual decline Bury presents as a case study in the triumph of rational thought over superstition, marking a decisive advance for intellectual liberty."
